policy_statement: Parent strategy: Green transport, aiming for zero carbon emissions from vehicles and the transport sector before 2050 through electrification, new energy transport, and traffic management measures.
mechanism_description: The strategy expects to reach zero-carbon transport by electrifying vehicles and ferries, developing new-energy transport, and implementing traffic management measures that reduce transport-sector emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Transport activity → electrification + cleaner energy + improved traffic flow → ↓ fossil-fuel combustion → ↓ CO₂e emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: Road and marine transport (vehicles, ferries, supporting operations) shift from internal combustion to electric/new-energy propulsion, and traffic is managed to reduce idling/congestion and unnecessary vehicle-kilometres.  
2) Mediating process: Electrification reduces tailpipe combustion; cleaner upstream electricity/fuels lower lifecycle emissions; traffic management improves average speeds and reduces stop‑and‑go conditions, decreasing fuel consumption per trip.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower CO₂ (and some non-CO₂) emissions from the transport sector, driving overall GHG mitigation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced transport-sector GHG emissions by substituting electricity/clean energy for fossil fuels and by cutting fuel burn through smoother/less traffic.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Transport system changes lead to less fossil-fuel combustion and therefore lower GHG emissions (mitigation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While traffic management can also improve local air quality and efficiency, and electrification can reduce energy intensity, the primary climate-relevant objective and impact across this mechanism is CO₂e reduction.  
3) Edge cases applied: If electrification is powered by high-carbon electricity, near-term net reductions may be smaller, but the mechanism’s intended dominant effect remains emissions reduction via decarbonized energy plus operational fuel savings; traffic management aimed purely at reducing congestion without cutting VKT could sometimes raise speeds and induce demand, but within this mechanism it is explicitly framed as emissions-reducing management.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy fitting “transport electrification and traffic management → transport emissions reduction” has the common primary outcome of reducing transport-sector GHGs.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether a given policy is best tagged as “vehicle electrification,” “marine electrification,” “congestion pricing,” “ITS optimization,” etc. depends on policy-level details and is not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential rebound/induced-demand effects from traffic improvements; lifecycle emissions depend on grid/fuel carbon intensity; some traffic management may target travel-time reliability rather than emissions unless explicitly designed (pricing, signal timing for smoothing, VKT reduction).
key_indicators: “Long-term target: Zero carbon emissions from vehicles and transport sector before 2050”; “achieve the long-term target”; “electrification of vehicles and ferries”; “development of new energy transport”; “measures to improve traffic management”
co_benefits: Improved air quality; reduced noise; reduced congestion/travel time; potential road safety improvements; energy security
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be read as a high-level planning strategy because it lists broad action areas, but the clearest instrument signal is the explicit “long-term target” framed around a 2050 endpoint.
